none: Walker's appeal : in four articles, together with a preamble to the colored citizens of the world : but in particular and very expressly to those of the United States of America
No known copyright restrictions. No known restrictions on use.

body.creator: LMEC Staff In & Out
body.value: Walker addresses his "Appeal" by explaining his first-person experience ...

body.creator: Jay
body.value:
Who has the right to revolt, and why?

The Haitian revolution (1791-1804) saw the first successful insurrection of enslaved Africans in the Americas. Formerly enslaved Haitians overthrew their colonial governments, ending a particularly brutal system of plantation slavery. After the revolution, Haiti immediately become an enduring symbol of hope -- of liberation, agency, and freedom -- to many Black Americans. For others, including white Southern planters and many of the nations founders, the Haitian Revolution was a source of profound alarm and a target of open hostility.

body.creator: NMC
body.value: The Declaration of Independence and Walker's Appeal are considered incendiary by some and a just call for action by others. How do they use language to inspire and provoke what they consider to be a fair rebellion?

body.creator: NMC
body.value: Walker compels the reader to use travel as a litmus test for freedom and points out the limits of independent movement for black people in the United States.

body.creator: NMC
body.value: Throughout the Appeal, Walker warns Americans of the hellfire that will result in what he perceives as their hypocritical Christianity. Walker uses italicized text and exclamation points to underscore the fire and brimstone he fears will be retributed on white Americans if they do not change their ways.

body.creator: Jay
body.value:
Rights for whom? Are not all men created equal?
Walker frames the struggle for Black freedom and racial equality within the language of the Declaration of Independence and, by extension, the American Revolution itself. Drawing on a tradition of Black abolitionist activism, Walker uses the Thomas Jefferson's own language about natural rights as both a moral and and political justification for Black resistance.
